summary:
Enmerkar and the Lord of Aratta is a Sumerian narrative poem, with its origins traceable to approximately 2800–2500 BC, recounting a legendary conflict and trade negotiation between Enmerkar, king of Uruk, and the unnamed lord of the distant highland city of Aratta. The composition is one of several epic narratives centering on the semi-legendary rulers of early Uruk and provides invaluable insight into early Sumerian concepts of kingship, divine mandate, commerce, and the geography of the known world.

In the poem, Enmerkar sends repeated messages to the lord of Aratta demanding tribute of precious metals, stones, and craftsmen for the embellishment of the Eanna temple of the goddess Inanna at Uruk. The poem contains what may be the first literary reference to writing, when the complexity of Enmerkar's message to Aratta exceeds what any messenger can memorise, and Enmerkar invents the clay tablet to convey it — a fascinating mythological reflection on the origins of cuneiform literacy.

Aratta has been tentatively identified by some scholars with sites in the Iranian plateau, possibly in the Jiroft region or further east, based on the poem's descriptions of its products (lapis lazuli, gold, silver, carnelian) and its distant, highland location. The poem's emphasis on commercial competition and the prestige of religious patronage reflects the real economic and political dynamics of the Early Dynastic period, when Sumerian city-states competed for control of trade routes and access to the exotic materials needed to demonstrate divine favour and royal magnificence.
